text:
The purpose of this study was to describe and explore the experiences and challenges of families caring for a child with a life limiting or life threatening condition from the time of diagnosis to bereavement.

Design: A qualitative study utilizing a grounded theory approach.

Methods: The study group consisted of 8 first-degree relatives (2 parents, 1 brother and 1 grandmother) from 5 families of individuals with ADM who provided care to the probands. Interviews were conducted face-to-face using semi-structured non-directed questions, with the aim of gaining a deeper understanding of their experiences and allowing narratives to emerge.

Abstract Background Over the past few decades, health care services for people with progressive diseases have undergone significant transformations. There has been a marked shift from the previous ‘family secret’ approach to one that is more open and compassionate. Objectives To describe and critically assess how a family copes with the experience of having a family member diagnosed with a progressive illness. Results Four main thematic categories were identified: 1) The experience of falling ill (diagnosis, disclosing and explaining the prognosis, and the family’s search for knowledge); 2) The caring phase (assimilating the demands of continuous care and its implications for the family); 3) Reconciling life with the disease (managing hope, feelings of guilt, and the cyclical roller coaster that the family experienced); and 4) Dying and death (entering a palliative care approach, ensuring a dignified death, and the immediate period following bereavement.

Conclusion: A serious illness of a child presents a families with a tremendous challenge, which directly affects all aspects of the family members’ lives. What families go through in such a situation is critically in need of intervention by means of comprehensive psychosocial support structures, professional counselling and a humanistic and transparent health care service to assist families in dealing with the many life changing shocks they experience.
